regions.label: Plato and Aristotle
regions.description: The two central figures walking beneath the main arch. Plato in red points upward to the realm of Forms, Aristotle in blue gestures outward toward the physical world.

regions.label: Heraclitus
regions.description: The brooding figure hunched alone over a marble block in the foreground, writing. Modeled after Michelangelo — Raphael painted his rival into the scene as tribute.

regions.label: Euclid
regions.description: Bent over in the lower right, drawing geometric figures with a compass on a slate while students crowd around to watch.

regions.label: Diogenes
regions.description: Sprawled casually on the steps in a teal robe, completely alone. The Cynic who rejected all material possessions and once told Alexander the Great to get out of his sunlight.

regions.label: Pythagoras
regions.description: Seated in the lower left, writing intently in a large book while students peer over his shoulder. The mathematician who believed the cosmos was built on harmony.

regions.label: Raphael, Ptolemy and Zoroaster
regions.description: Far right: Ptolemy holds a terrestrial globe, Zoroaster a celestial one. Raphael painted himself just behind them — the only figure looking directly at the viewer.

beats.narration: One painting. Fifty philosophers. Two thousand years of human thought, frozen in a single room.
At the center: the argument that started everything. Plato points up — truth is abstract. Aristotle pushes down — truth is here, in the world.
Sitting alone, ignoring everyone. This is Heraclitus — or rather, Michelangelo. Raphael painted his greatest rival into the scene as tribute.
Diogenes. Sprawled on the steps like he owns the place. He once told Alexander the Great to move — he was blocking the sun.
Pythagoras, lost in his notebook. The man who heard music in mathematics and believed the universe was built on harmony.
Euclid draws a perfect circle while his students lean in. Geometry — the one language that never lies.
Ptolemy holds the earth. Zoroaster holds the stars. And just behind them — Raphael himself, the only figure looking straight at you.
Every figure here shaped the world. And Raphael put them all in one room — because great ideas never exist alone.
